coredata.dc:title: Construction of integrated and portable fluorescence sensor and the application for visual detection in situ
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Haibo
Wang, Yumin
Zhang, Li
Cao, Yujuan
Guo, Manli
Yu, Ying
Lin, Bixia
coredata.dc:description: The development of portable and low-cost fluorescence sensors is significant for on-site detection. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensor integrating power source, light source, filter, sample cell and smartphone was constructed. The model of fluorescence sensor was designed by using Autodesk Computer Aided Design (CAD) software, and printed with 3D printing technology, followed by assembling with various components. With advantages of convenient carrying, good stability, wide applicability and low cost, the fluorescence sensor showed great adaptability for on-site visual detection. Nitrite was chosen as the model of analyte, and ratiometric fluorescent probe (B/R-CDs) was employed as the identification and signal element for detecting nitrite. With the increase of nitrite concentration, the fluorescence intensity of B/R-CDs at 460 nm decreased, while that at 615 nm kept stable. Therefore, the fluorescence color of B/R-CDs changed from blue to red. By using the portable sensor to collect RGB value of fluorescence color, the R/B channel values showed a linear relationship with nitrite concentration in 2.5–600 µM, with detection limit of 0.8 µM. Then the fluorescence sensor was used to detect nitrite in preserved meat and pickles, and recovery rate was among 94.0%−105%. The detection results were consistent with those of fluorescence method and spectrophotometry. This work provides a novel detection device for on-site analysis, and also offers an effective approach for developing miniature fluorescence sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portability
Fluorescence sensor
Visualization
On-site detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of nitrite by portable fluorescence sensor
Determination of nitrite in real samples
Design and construction of portable fluorescence sensor
Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs and sensing mechanism of nitrite
Detection of nitrite by portable fluorescence sensor
Selectivity and reproducibility of nitrite detection
Determination of nitrite in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design of integrated and portable fluorescence sensor.(A) Appearance of the fluorescence sensor. (B) Diagram of the internal optical path. (C) Entity diagrams of components and accessories.
Stability of the fluorescence sensor.(A) The intra-day stability. (B) The inter-day stability. The stability in the presence (C) or absence (D) of copper sheet.

Effect of nitrite on the fluorescence of B-CDs, R-CDs and B/R-CDs.Fluorescence spectra of (A) B-CDs, (B) R-CDs and (D) B/R-CDs with nitrite at different concentrations. (C) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, R-CDs and B/R-CDs. The inset shows photos under UV lamp of 365 nm.
Sensing mechanism of nitrite by B-CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption and (B) FT-IR spectra of B-CDs before and after adding nitrite. N1s XPS spectrum of B-CDs before (C) and after (D) adding nitrite.

Detection of nitrite by portable fluorescence sensor. (A) Detection interface of smartphone. (B) Photos of B/R-CDs with different concentrations of nitrite. (C) The linear relationship between R/B values and nitrite concentrations.
The integrated and portable fluorescence sensor for visual detection of nitrite by the aid of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs.
Determination of nitrite in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Construction of integrated and portable fluorescence sensor and the application for visual detection in situ

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of portable and low-cost fluorescence sensors is significant for on-site detection. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensor integrating power source, light source, filter, sample cell and smartphone was constructed. The model of fluorescence sensor was designed by using Autodesk Computer Aided Design (CAD) software, and printed with 3D printing technology, followed by assembling with various components. With advantages of convenient carrying, good stability, wide applicability and low cost, the fluorescence sensor showed great adaptability for on-site visual detection. Nitrite was chosen as the model of analyte, and ratiometric fluorescent probe (B/R-CDs) was employed as the identification and signal element for detecting nitrite. With the increase of nitrite concentration, the fluorescence intensity of B/R-CDs at 460 nm decreased, while that at 615 nm kept stable. Therefore, the fluorescence color of B/R-CDs changed from blue to red. By using the portable sensor to collect RGB value of fluorescence color, the R/B channel values showed a linear relationship with nitrite concentration in 2.5–600 µM, with detection limit of 0.8 µM. Then the fluorescence sensor was used to detect nitrite in preserved meat and pickles, and recovery rate was among 94.0%−105%. The detection results were consistent with those of fluorescence method and spectrophotometry. This work provides a novel detection device for on-site analysis, and also offers an effective approach for developing miniature fluorescence sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional laboratory methods are not suitable for on-site rapid assays because they usually require cumbersome equipment and operation by well-trained professionals. In particular, the cost of equipment will greatly limit the application of these traditional methods in less developed regions and countries. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a portable and low-cost device for on-site detection. In the past few decade, the development of a variety of micro sensors, including microfluidic chips [1,2], ambient light sensors (ALS) [3,4] and electrochemical sensors [5,6], has aroused great interests in various fields, such as food safety [7–10], environment surveillance [11–13] and clinical diagnosis [14,15]. Due to the advantages of high sensitivity, simple operation, and quick response, fluorescence analysis methods have been widely used for detecting metal ions [16–18], pesticide residues [19–21], disease markers [22–24], etc. However, fluorescence methods mainly rely on fluorescence spectrometer [25]. Even the visual detection based on fluorescence signal still depends on ultraviolet (UV) lamp powered by 220 V power and dark environment [19]. As a consequence, these assays can only be performed in the laboratory and their potential application in on-site detection is significantly restricted.

In recent years, 3D printing emerges as a new technology to produce highly complex and integrated multi-functional accessories [26]. Benefiting from the low cost and recyclable features, 3D printing is introduced to produce fluorescence sensor devices with a dark box [14,19,22]. For example, Su et al. used 3D printing technology to design a sensing platform for precise quantification of 2, 4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid based on the red emissive CDs/CoOOH composite [8]. Xiao et al. developed a paper-based microarray integrating fluorescent CDs and a smartphone-based reader for quantitation of multiple heavy metal ions [27]. However, the reported devices still confront the following limitations: (1) 220 V power is essential, which restricts the convenient use. (2) When the battery is used as the power source, the current will decrease with the discharge time, which may cause the instability of light source. (3) LED lamp is prone to produce heat, and insufficient heat dissipation may cause wavelength shift, leading to inaccurate detection result or even burning down of LED lamp. (4) The devices are usually suitable for only one type of mobile phone, exhibiting limited applicability.

Herein, in order to overcome the above deficiencies, multiple strategies were introduced to design a novel fluorescence sensor. Specifically, a portable power bank was employed as the power supply of the device, and copper sheet with excellent heat dissipation performance was adopted to assist the heat dissipation of LED. Meanwhile, the adjustable bracket was designed to adapt to different specifications of mobile phones. The designed fluorescence sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1. The sensor integrates power supply, light source, filter, sample cell and mobile phone, showing the advantages of portability and easy operation. To explore the practicability of the fluorescence sensor, the visual detection of nitrite, a kind of food additive and preservative, was performed on the fluorescence sensor with the aid of a ratiometric fluorescent probe (B/R-CDs). When nitrite was present, nitrite reacted with the amino group on B-CDs to generate diazo compounds, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs. On the other hand, R-CDs acted as a reference signal. With the increase of nitrite concentrations, the fluorescence color of B/R-CDs gradually changed from blue to red. The RGB value of the fluorescence color was captured and readout by the camera and color recognition application in smartphone. The relationship between the RGB value and the nitrite concentration was applied for visual detection of nitrite.

m-Phenylenediamine (m-PD) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid (85%) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Anhydrous ethanol and dichloromethane were provided by Sun Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Preserved meat was purchased from Tmall supermarket. Pickle was obtained by homemade method.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra were measured by using a UV-2700 ultraviolet spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra assays were performed with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) under the voltage of 550 V, and slit width of 10 nm for both excitation and emission. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by using a JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a spectrum two Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Platinum Elmer, German). XPS characterization was performed by Thermo Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an FLS1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The 3D model of portable fluorescence sensor was designed using AutoCAD 2007 software. Minitree V2.0 3D printer was used for 3D printing. Power bank with model of ONDA Q100T and current output of 2: DC5V/2.0 A was produced by Guangzhou Zhile Electronic Technology Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China). The UV lamp (365 nm) was obtained from Yuhua Instrument Co., Ltd (Gongyi, China). The LED lamp (365 nm) was purchased from Guanghong. The optical filter was produced by Beijing Yongxing Sensing Instrument., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All photos were taken with Huawei Honor 9 smartphone. The color recognition application was developed by Xiyi Technology.

Firstly, the dimensions of optical and electronic components were built with AutoCAD 2007 software. Then the corresponding 3D models for each component were printed by the aid of a 3D printer. The LED lamp with welded resistance, optical filter, copper sheet were embedded into optical source groove, followed by assembling with optical cassette, photon shutter, sample cell, shading cover and other accessories. Finally, the smartphone was installed to the adjustable bracket, and the LED lamp was connected to the power bank. In this way, an integrated and portable fluorescent sensor was successfully constructed.

B-CDs were synthesized according to a reported method with some modification [28]. Briefly, 0.1080 g of m-PD and 250 µL of phosphoric acid (85%) were mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water under stirring. The mixture was sealed into a 50 mL of teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, and the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 10 min. The supernatant was then collected and filtered with 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain a brown-black solution, which was stored at 4 ℃ under dark condition for further utilization.

R-CDs were prepared according to the literature with modification [29]. 0.3000 g of p-PD was dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to 100 mL of teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was purified with a silica chromatography column using mixture of dichloromethane and ethanol as eluent. The final product was dried in rotary evaporator, and re-dispersed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol. The obtained R-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.
For the subsequent experiments, the ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs was obtained by mixing B-CDs and R-CDs according to a fluorescence intensity ratio of 4:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite by portable fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In a series of colorimetric tubes, 200 µL of B/R-CDs probe solution and 500 µL of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane-hydrochloric acid buffer solution (Tris-HCl, 10 mM, pH=6.0) were mixed. Subsequently, different volumes of nitrite solution were separately added into the above solution and diluted to 2.0 mL with ultrapure water. The final concentrations of nitrite in each solution were 0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500 and 600 µM. After reacting at room temperature for 30 min, each solution was detected by using the portable fluorescence sensor. The smartphone captured the photos of the fluorescence signal from detection system, and the color recognition application read out the RGB values. The calibration curve was established between RGB values and nitrite concentrations. Meanwhile, the fluorescence method was also established for comparison. The fluorescence spectrum of each solution was measured at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the relationship was established between fluorescence intensity ratio and nitrite concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real samples were pretreated based on the National Standard for Food Safety (GB5009.33–2016). Briefly, 5.00 g of the sample homogenate was added in a 150 mL of conical flask, followed by adding 80 mL of water. The homogenate was ultrasonically extracted for 30 min, accompanying with shaking once every 5 min to keep the solid phase completely dispersed. Afterwards, the mixture was transferred into water bath at 75 ℃ for 5 min, and then diluted to 100 mL in a volumetric flask with water. The solution was filtered by using filter paper, and the filtrate was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 15 min. The supernatant was further filtered by 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain the final filtrate for detection. For the recovery experiments, the real samples were added with different concentrations of nitrite solution, and then pretreated by following the same steps mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of portable fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional fluorescence methods mostly rely on fluorescence spectrometer. In order to develop a portable fluorescence device suitable for on-site assays, a miniature and integrated fluorescence sensor was designed. The basic structure of traditional optical instrument is mainly composed of light source, monochromator, sample cell, detector and data processor. Inspired by this, in the structural design of the fluorescence sensor, the commercial filter was adopted for light splitting, and quartz cuvette was used as the sample cell. Additionally, both the data monitoring and processing were undertaken by smartphone. The smartphone camera captured the fluorescence signal and converted it to image. The color recognition application identified and output the RGB value of the image for the quantitative analysis of analyte. For light source, low-cost LED lamp with adjustable wavelength was used, which effectively avoided the limit of 220 V power supply.

Moreover, to promote the integration and improve the suitability of the fluorescence sensor, the modular design was performed prior to assembly of the fluorescence sensor. The main modules were listed as follow: optical cassette for dark environment, light source groove for loading LED lamp and optical filter, sample cell which was matched with cuvette, and an adjustable bracket suitable for holding all kinds of smartphone. In addition, there were also some accessories such as photon shutter, cuvette lid, shading cover, rotating screw nut, movable bolt, screw, spring, resistances, wiring terminal, USB interface, copper sheet, etc. To ensure that each module matched the corresponding component and were able to combine into a whole by accessories, various types of LED lamp, optical filter and cuvette were compared to select the optimal specification. Then, AutoCAD software was used to meticulously design the parameters of each module and accessory according to the specification of each component. The final parameters were shown in Fig. S1. Subsequently, as showed in Fig. 1, the models of each module and accessory were printed by means of 3D printing technology and assembled into an integrated fluorescence sensor. The installation process was provided in Video S1.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132764.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1
Video S1
.

In the designed fluorescence sensor, the functions of each component were listed as follow: Optical cassette was used for providing dark detection environment. Light source groove acted for loading LED lamp, optical filter, resistance and copper sheet. The filter allowed only ultraviolet light at 365 nm to pass through. The resistance played the role of current and partial pressure limit to prevent the LED lamp from bearing excessive load. The copper sheet promoted heat dissipation. Quartz cuvette was placed in the sample cell for holding sample solution. The cuvette lid and the shading cover acted as light shield. The rotating screw nut, movable bolt, screw, and spring were used for constructing adjustable bracket which was suitable for holding various smartphones with camera locating at different positions. Wiring terminal was used for connecting LED lamp and resistance to power bank. The USB interface was employed for the access of the power bank which provided stable voltage for the LED lamp. Smartphone was applied to take photos and readout the RGB values for analysis.

Before the further application, the stability of the fluorescence sensor was investigated firstly. The blue and red fluorescent carbon dots with good stability were selected for the investigation. The intra-day and inter-day fluorescence colors and RGB data of carbon dots were collected by using the fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, for both the blue and red carbon dots, no obvious difference was observed in the color and RGB data obtained at different time intra-day and in different days. The results showed that the fluorescence sensor had good stability. In addition, copper sheet was added in the fluorescence sensor to accelerate the heat dissipation of LED lamp. The role of copper sheet in maintaining the stability of the device was also studied in Fig. 2C and D. In the presence of copper sheet, the fluorescence color and RGB value kept unchanged when carbon dots were irradiated continuously for 30 min. However, in the absence of copper sheet, the fluorescence color faded completely after 5 min, and the connecting wire was fused in the 9th minute, accompanying the burning down of LED lamp (Fig. S2). This arose from the reason that heat produced from LED lamp could not timely dissipate, and accumulated to rise the temperature sharply. Conversely, in the case of copper sheet, copper sheet acted as an excellent heat-transfer medium to reduce the temperature of LED lamp and finally improve the stability of the device.

Compared with some fluorescence detection devices listed in Table S1, the fluorescence sensor developed here possessed the following advantages: (1) Ultra-portability. The device was small in size (length × width × height, 8.0 cm × 7.5 cm × 3.0 cm), and light in weight (92 g). It adopted a portable charger as the power source, which further improved the portability. (2) Good stability. Compared with battery, power bank provided long-term stable current. Meanwhile, the use of copper sheet solved the heat dissipation problem to avoid the wavelength shift of LED lamp caused by heat accumulation. (3) Wide applicability. Benefiting from the adjustable bracket, the device was suitable for all types of smartphone. (4) Low cost. The cost of whole device except for smartphone was about 65 CNY ($9.6) which was lower than many reported fluorescence devices. Moreover, because the device could be produced in quantity through simple 3D printing, the device could be commercially produced for widespread use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs and sensing mechanism of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical performance, the fluorescence sensor was applied for the detection of nitrite. Prior to detection, a fluorescent probe that could visually respond to nitrite should be designed. To achieve this, two kinds of carbon dots were explored (Figs. S3-S6). The blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) synthesized by m-PD could be efficiently quenched by nitrite ( Fig. 3A). When the nitrite concentration is 400 µM, the quenching efficiency reached more than 95%. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence color disappeared from blue gradually. Moreover, the fluorescence of red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) synthesized by p-PD showed little change with the addition of nitrite, and the fluorescence color kept unchanged (Fig. 3B). Generally, the double emission probe can present rich fluorescence color, which can improve the sensitivity and accuracy of visual detection. Since nitrite could quench the fluorescence of B-CDs, while it had no effect on that of R-CDs, thus, the ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs was developed for nitrite detection by using B-CDs as the indicator signal and R-CDs as the reference signal, respectively. Under the excitation of 365 nm, B/R-CDs maintained the emission peaks of B-CDs at 460 nm and R-CDs at 615 nm (Fig. 3C). With the increase of nitrite concentration, the fluorescence at 460 nm was gradually decreased, while that at 615 nm basically remained unchanged, accompanying the fluorescence color change from blue to red (Fig. 3D).

In order to explore the sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent probe B/R-CDs for nitrite, UV–vis absorption spectra, FT-IR spectra, XPS characterization and fluorescence lifetime measurement were performed. In Fig. 4A, the characteristic absorption peaks of B-CDs at 244 nm and 295 nm were blue shifted to 235 nm and 286 nm in the presence of nitrite. Meanwhile, a significant new absorption peak appeared at 458 nm, implying that B-CDs had interaction with nitrite. In Fig. 4B, for the FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs, characteristic peaks at 3450 cm−1 (O-H, ν), 3240 cm−1 (N-H, ν), 2824 cm−1 (C-H, ν), 1634 cm−1 (-NH2, δ) [30] and 1060 cm−1 (C-N, ν) were observed. Moreover, CC skeleton vibration of aromatic ring at 1519 cm−1and C-H plane bending vibration of C-N aromatic ring at 940 cm−1 were also displayed clearly, manifesting that B-CDs contained hydroxyl, amino, conjugated double bond and aromatic ring. After the addition of nitrite, a new peak appeared at 2087 cm−1, which was the cumulative tensile vibration peak of triple bond or double bond [28]. The N1s XPS spectra of B-CDs before and after the nitrite addition were displayed in Fig. 4C and D. The N element of B-CDs mainly existed in the groups of graphene nitrogen, pyrrole and amino. After the addition of nitrite, the amino group converted to C-NN, which proved that diazotization occurred [28,31]. The fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs kept basically unchanged after adding nitrite (Fig. S7), which further testified that the fluorescence quenching was caused by the formation of non-luminescent compounds between B-CDs and nitrite. In Figs. S8, no significant changes were observed in fluorescence intensity at 615 nm, fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis absorption and FT-IR spectra of R-CDs after adding nitrite under acidic condition, indicating that no reaction happened between nitrite and R-CDs. Therefore, R-CDs were suitable as reference signal in the ratiometric fluorescence probe. The detection mechanism of B/R-CDs for nitrite was shown in Fig. S9. The detection conditions were optimized in Figs. S10 and S11. The optimal initial fluorescence intensity ratio of B-CDs and R-CDs was 4:1, and the optimal pH value and reaction time were 6 and 30 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite by portable fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Next, with the assistance of probe B/R-CDs, the feasibility of the portable fluorescence sensor for visual detection of nitrite was investigated. B/R-CDs with different concentrations of nitrite were placed into sample cell. Then the smartphone was used to collect the pictures of fluorescence signals and converted them to RGB values for establishing linear relationship with the concentration of nitrite. The specific operation was supplied as Video S2. The captured photos of detection systems were shown in Figs. 5A and B. As can be seen, with the increase of nitrite concentration, the color of the detection system changed gradually from blue to red, enabling visual semi-quantitative detection of nitrite by the naked eye. The relationship between red channel value/blue channel value (R/B value) and nitrite concentration was established in Fig. 5C. The R/B value enhanced with the increase of nitrite concentration, and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 2.5–600 µM. The linear equation was y = 0.00256x + 0.560 (x: concentration of nitrite, µM), and the correlation coefficient R 2 was 0.995. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.8 µM (3σ/S).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132764.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2
Video S2
.

The working curve of detecting nitrite by fluorescence method was also established based on B/R-CDs probe for verifying the reliability of portable fluorescence sensor. In Fig. S12, it was found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 615/F 460) exhibited a good linear relationship to the concentration of nitrite in two ranges of 1–120 µM and 120–600 µM, respectively. The linear equations were F 615/F 460 = 0.00271 × CNitrite + 0.267 and F 615/F 460 = 0.00463 × CNitrite + 0.0396, while the correlation coefficients R 2 were 0.999 and 0.994, respectively. In addition, the LOD was calculated to be 0.36 µM (3σ/S). By compared with some other fluorescence or colorimetric methods in Table S2, the detection methods for nitrite established in this study exhibited wider linear range and better sensitivity. Moreover, the detection of only using B-CDs was presented in Fig. S13. The results indicated the detection by using B-CDs had higher LOD, narrower linear range and poorer visualization than that by using B/R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: Because the selectivity of the fluorescence sensor towards analyte mainly depends on that of fluorescence probe, the selectivity of fluorescent probe B/R-CDs for nitrite detection was investigated. Some substances that may exist in preserved meat and pickles including ascorbic acid, alanine, cysteine, glucose, glutathione, some oxidizing substances such as hydrogen peroxide, and some ions such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, NH4 +, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, CO3 2- were chosen for the investigation. As shown in Fig. S14, the F 615/F 460 values after the addition of other molecules or ions were basically the same as that of the blank group. Only nitrite caused obvious increase of F 615/F 460 values, and copper ion caused a certain increase. According to the photos taken under UV lamp, the fluorescence color kept blue after the addition of various molecules and ions, except that nitrite and copper ion led to red color. Besides, the anti-interference ability of B/R-CDs for nitrite detection was investigated in Fig. S15. Similarly, only copper ion had interference on the detection. However, the interference of copper ion could be effectively eliminated by using sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) to form coordination compound with copper ion (Fig. S16) [32]. All the results indicated that the fluorescence probe B/R-CDs had good selectivity and anti-interference in detecting nitrite and it could be applied for analysis of nitrite in complex samples.

In order to investigate the reproducibility of the portable fluorescence sensor for nitrite detection, the intra-day and inter-day tests were performed. As shown in Fig. S17, both the fluorescence color and the RGB value of blank B/R-CDs solution and B/R-CDs with nitrite remained unchanged when tested in every two hours. The similar results were also found in inter-day test. All the results verified that the reproducibility of detecting nitrite by the portable fluorescence sensor was good.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicability of the portable fluorescence sensor for the on-site detection of actual samples, pickles and preserved meat were determined. The detection results were also compared with the fluorescence method established in this work and the spectrophotometry according to National Standard (GB 5009.33–2016). As shown in Table 1, the residual nitrite in pickles and preserved meat measured by the portable fluorescence sensor were 6.4 µM and 45.9 µM, respectively, which were similar to those measured by fluorescence method and spectrophotometry. For the samples spiked with 10, 50 and 100 µM nitrite, the detection results obtained by these three methods were consistent, and the recoveries were 94.0%− 105%, 92.6%− 109%, and 92.2%− 116%, respectively. The results clearly showed that the fluorescence sensor had good accuracy and practicality.

In summary, a novel fluorescence sensor integrated with power source, light source, filter, sample cell and smartphone was designed. The fluorescence sensor had the advantages of portability, good stability, wide applicability and low cost, showing great practicality for the on-site visual detection of analyte. Nitrite was selected as the model of analyte and was detected by using this fluorescence sensor based on a ratiometric fluorescence probe B/R-CDs. The R/B value exhibited a good linear relationship with the nitrite concentration in the range of 2.5–600 µM. The limit of detection was 0.8 µM which was much lower than the maximum allowed limit in drinking water (65 µM) proposed by World Health Organization (WHO). The fluorescence sensor was also used for the detection of pickles and preserved meat. The results confirmed the good accuracy and reproducibility of the fluorescence sensor. The fluorescence sensor exhibited a promising application prospect for point-of-care testing in food safety and environment surveillance especially in less developed regions and countries. More importantly, the design philosophy of the portable fluorescence sensor provided a reliable pathway for the development of miniature fluorescence sensors.

Haibo Liang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing − original draft. Yumin Wang: Writing − review & editing. Li Zhang: Writing − review & editing. Manli Guo: Resources. Yujuan Cao: Resources. Ying Yu: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing, Project administration. Bixia Lin: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22004039, 52070080, 21575043), Education Department of Guangdong Province (No. 2020KTSCX033 and 2019KQNCX027), Guangzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 202102080612 and 202102020522).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132764.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haibo Liang is a graduate student under the supervision of professor Ying Yu and Dr. Bixia Lin in South China Normal University. His research interest focuses on preparation of fluorescent nanomaterials and chemical sensing methods.

Yumin Wang is currently an associate research fellow of analytical chemistry in Guangxi Normal University. He obtained Ph.D degree from Hunan University in 2017. His research interest focuses on chemical and biological sensing methods.

Li Zhang is currently an associate research fellow in Nanchang Hangkong University. He obtained Ph.D degree from Sun Yat-sen University in 2017. His research interest focuses on electrochemical sensing technology.

Yujuan Cao is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry in South China Normal University. She obtained Ph.D degree from Chinese Academy of Science in 2005. Her research interest focuses on preparation and application of functional nanomaterials.

Manli Guo is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry in South China Normal University. She obtained Ph.D degree from Hunan University in 2007. Her research interest focuses on chemical sensing technology.

Ying Yu is currently a professor of analytical chemistry in South China Normal University. She received Master degree (1989) from Jiangxi University and Ph.D degree (2005) from South China University of Technology. Her research interest focuses on the design, synthesis and application of fluorescent probes, as well as chemical and biological sensing technology.

Bixia Lin obtained Ph.D degree from South China Normal University in 2019. Her research interest focuses on chemical and biological sensing methods, preparation and application of functional nanomaterials.
